paper_metadata.title: Determinants of public support for transport taxes
paper_metadata.authors: Steffen Kallbekken, Jorge H. Garcia, Kristine Korneliussen
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Online survey of adults living in or near Oslo, Bergen, and Trondheim; final sample N=751.
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Directly analyzes public support for fuel taxes on gasoline and diesel in Norway using referendum-style survey questions about maintaining, reducing, or increasing the tax.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived fuel-tax effectiveness in improving air quality
determinants.definition: Belief that increasing fuel taxes would improve local air quality.
determinants.mechanism: Seeing the tax as effective at reducing pollution increases acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).

determinants.definition: Belief that fuel taxes hurt low-income households.
determinants.mechanism: Greater perceived regressivity lowers fairness and support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
determinants.evidence_quote: The variable distributional effect is negative and highly significant across the three models.

determinants.definition: Belief that fuel taxes hurt one's own finances.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
determinants.evidence_quote: the variable personal finance is negative and highly significant across the three instruments.

determinants.definition: Belief that good public transport alternatives are available locally.
determinants.mechanism: Better alternatives reduce car dependence and make fuel taxes less burdensome.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
determinants.evidence_quote: A one unit increase in the variable transport alternatives increases the likelihood of support of fuel taxes by 1.9% points.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Fuel-tax support +1.9 percentage points; significant at 10%.

determinants.definition: Belief that authorities spend tax and fee revenues sensibly.
determinants.mechanism: Trust in government revenue use increases support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).

determinants.definition: General concern about harms from driving.
determinants.mechanism: Greater concern for external harms increases support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
determinants.evidence_quote: The variable concern driving is, as expected, consistently positive across the three taxes.

determinants.mechanism: Women in this sample are less supportive of fuel taxes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
determinants.evidence_quote: Being female reduces the probability of acceptance of fuel taxes

determinants.definition: Indicator for having a university or college degree.
determinants.mechanism: Higher education is associated with greater support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Norwegian fuel tax on gasoline and diesel; support for maintaining or increasing the existing tax (NOK 5/litre).
determinants.evidence_quote: having a university degree increases the likelihood of acceptance of these instruments
